summary:
Enamel lacks self-repair capability primarily because its forming cells disappear after complete development; without active cell populations, natural restoration mechanisms aren’t possible. Additionally, being largely non-living crystal structure akin to other avascular tissues also contributes by limiting access to nutrients essential for any reparatory process through vascular systems absent from such hard surfaces. To directly answer your query within two concise statements:

Enamel can't self-repair mainly as its initial-forming specialized cells vanish post-enamel completion leaving no intrinsic means for renewal or recovery. Moreover, lacking both vasculature typical in soft-tissues hinders availability of vital resources needed during regenerative phases further impeding inherent mending capacities observed elsewhere in our body anatomy. ```plaintext
Enamel cannot self-repair because its specific forming cells cease functioning after tooth mineralization, and since it does not have blood vessels, it lacks the ability to receive crucial nutrients required for repairs.
```
